response: Penicillin, first noticed by Alexander Fleming in 1928, launched an antibiotic revolution that made previously lethal infections treatable and enabled safe surgery, cancer chemotherapy, and organ transplants. A “golden age” in the 1950s–60s produced most of the 20-plus antibiotic classes we still rely on, but pharmaceutical investment later dried up because short treatment courses yield lower profits than chronic-disease drugs, so only a handful of new classes have reached the market in the past three decades. Meanwhile, heavy hospital use and routine inclusion of antibiotics in livestock feed have driven a global surge of resistant pathogens such as MRSA, creating a public-health crisis the WHO ranks alongside climate change. To counter resistance, researchers are revisiting bacteriophage therapy, experimenting with CRISPR-based precision antimicrobials, and using AI to screen billions of compounds in hopes of jump-starting the stalled antibiotic pipeline.
